title: Why forgiveness is not required for recovery
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
We've been told a lie about forgiveness

I remember sitting in a support group years ago, listening to a woman describe the abuse she'd survived. Her voice was quiet, careful. She talked about nightmares that still woke her at 3am. About flinching when her partner touched her shoulder. About the rage she carried in her chest like a hot stone she couldn't swallow.

And then the group leader said something I'll never forget. "You need to forgive him. For yourself. Forgiveness is the only way to heal."

I watched this woman's face crumple. She nodded. Said she'd try harder. But I saw what happened in her body. Her shoulders curved inward. Her breath went shallow. She was swallowing another layer of shame on top of everything she already carried.

Here's the thing. That advice was wrong. Not just unhelpful. Actually harmful.

The idea that forgiveness is required for recovery is one of the most damaging myths the wellness industry has sold us. It lands on people who are already drowning and tells them they're not doing enough. That there's one more spiritual hurdle they haven't cleared. One more reason their pain is their fault.

I want to say this as clearly as I can. You do not have to forgive. Not today. Not ever. And your recovery can still be real, deep, and complete without it.

Where the forgiveness mandate comes from

Let's be honest about who benefits from the "you must forgive" narrative. It's not survivors. It's systems that want us quiet. Families that don't want to be uncomfortable. Religious institutions that prioritize unity over justice. A culture that finds our anger inconvenient.

Dr. Judith Herman, who literally wrote the book on trauma recovery, has spent decades studying what survivors actually need. Her research is clear. Recovery happens in stages. First comes safety. Then remembrance and mourning. Then reconnection with ordinary life. Forgiveness? Not on the list. Not once.

Let me be honest with you about something. Recovery is expensive. Not just financially - though therapy and bodywork and supplements and books add up fast. It's expensive in time, in energy, in relationships. Some people won't understand why you're changing. Some people benefited from your old patterns and they won't like the new ones. That's real. And it's painful.

But here's what's also real. The cost of not healing is higher. It's paid in health - in autoimmune conditions, in chronic pain, in shortened lifespan. It's paid in relationships - in repeating the same patterns, in choosing the same unavailable partners, in pushing away the people who could actually love you. It's paid in aliveness - in years spent numb, disconnected, going through the motions.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying it because the choice to heal isn't a luxury. It's a necessity. And it's one that only you can make. No one can do this work for you. But you don't have to do it alone. That's the paradox of recovery - it's deeply personal work that requires other people. You need witnesses. You need co-regulators. You need at least one person who can see you clearly and not look away. Find that person. Or let them find you.

In her foundational work Trauma and Recovery (paid link), Herman describes how survivors who are pushed toward premature forgiveness often experience a worsening of symptoms. They dissociate more. Their shame deepens. They lose trust in their own perceptions. Because forgiveness before you've fully acknowledged what happened isn't healing. It's bypassing.

Stay with me here. The pressure to forgive often comes from people who haven't done their own work. People who are uncomfortable with your pain because it reminds them of their own. People who want you to be "over it" so they don't have to witness your suffering anymore.

That's not your problem to solve. Your recovery is not about making other people comfortable.

What happens when you force forgiveness

I've worked with survivors who spent years trying to forgive. They went to workshops. They read books. They lit candles and said affirmations. And every time they couldn't reach that place of genuine forgiveness, they added another layer of failure to their already heavy load.

Think about it. You're already carrying the original wound. The betrayal. The violation. The loss. Then you're told that healing requires you to reach a specific emotional state. And when you can't get there, you blame yourself. That's not healing. That's retraumatization wearing spiritual clothing.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, whose research at the Trauma Center in Boston has shaped how we understand the body's role in trauma, talks about this directly. He's observed that forcing forgiveness before the nervous system has fully processed the threat can actually lock trauma responses deeper into the body. Your system reads the pressure to forgive as another form of coercion. Another demand to override your own signals.

Wild, right? The very thing you're told will set you free can actually keep you stuck.

I've seen this pattern so many times. Someone comes in saying "I've forgiven them but I still have nightmares." Or "I forgave years ago but I still can't stand to be touched." The forgiveness was cognitive. It was a decision made in the neocortex. But the body doesn't care about decisions. The body remembers. And until the body feels safe, the trauma isn't resolved.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana, a clinician who studied directly with Stephen Porges. She breaks down how polyvagal theory applies to real life. Not bypassing. Not forcing. Just understanding what your nervous system actually needs to settle.

The alternative to forgiveness is not bitterness

Let me be really clear about something. Not forgiving does not mean staying stuck in rage. It does not mean rehearsing grievances every day. It does not mean refusing to move forward with your life.

What it means is this. You get to decide what relationship you have with the past. You get to acknowledge that what happened was wrong. You get to feel your anger fully without being told it's a lower vibration. You get to protect yourself without having to forgive the person who hurt you.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He writes about the difference between forgiveness and acceptance. Acceptance is about reality. What happened, happened. You can accept that without forgiving anyone. You can accept that someone was incapable of giving you what you needed without letting them off the hook.

Here's what I've found in my own recovery. The more I stopped trying to forgive, the more space I had for other things. Grief. Anger. Curiosity about my own patterns. Real connection with people who actually saw me. The forgiveness project was taking up so much energy. When I dropped it, I had energy for what actually mattered.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Some of you felt relief. Some of you felt fear. Some of you felt a quiet "but I'm supposed to..." That's the programming. That's the cultural conditioning. And you get to question it.

I'm not saying forgiveness is bad. For some people, it happens naturally over time. But it's a result of healing, not a requirement for it. And for many survivors, genuine forgiveness never comes. That's not a failure. That's a honest response to what happened.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about accepting what is, including the parts of us that aren't ready to forgive. Including the parts that are still angry. Including the parts that may never forgive. That's real acceptance. Not the kind that bypasses pain, but the kind that holds it.

What recovery actually requires

So if forgiveness isn't required, what is? I can tell you what the research shows and what I've seen in my own life.

First, safety. Your nervous system needs to know, in its bones, that the threat is over. This takes time. It takes consistency. It takes environments and relationships that don't trigger your survival responses.

Second, acknowledgment. Someone needs to validate what happened. Ideally the person who hurt you. But often that's not possible. So you need witnesses. People who believe you. People who say "yes, that was wrong" without adding "but you should forgive."

Third, mourning. You have to grieve what you lost. The childhood you didn't have. The trust that was broken. The years you spent surviving instead of living. Grief is not a problem to solve. It's a process to move through.

Fourth, choice. Real recovery means you get to choose your relationship with the past. Not because someone told you to. But because you've done enough work that the choice is actually yours. Some days you might feel compassion for the person who hurt you. Other days you might feel rage. Both are valid. Both are part of being a living, breathing human with a nervous system that remembers.

I want to say something that might be hard to hear. Some people never fully forgive. And they heal anyway. They build good lives. They love deeply. They find meaning. They just don't forgive. And that's okay.

Think about it. If forgiveness was the only path, what does that say about the millions of survivors who can't get there? That they're doomed? That they'll never heal? That's not a compassionate framework. That's spiritual coercion dressed up as wisdom.

You get to decide

I'm not here to tell you what to do with your own forgiveness journey. Maybe it comes naturally to you. Maybe it never does. Maybe it comes and goes. Maybe you forgive some people and not others. Maybe you forgive in your heart but maintain distance in your life.

All of those are valid. All of those can coexist with real, lasting recovery.

What I'm asking you to do is question the narrative. Question who benefits from you forgiving quickly. Question whether the pressure you feel is coming from your own heart or from a culture that doesn't want to sit with your pain.

Your anger is not a problem to fix. Your refusal to forgive is not a spiritual failing. Your body's resistance is not a sign you're doing it wrong.

Sometimes the most healing thing you can do is stop trying to heal the way you're told you should. Sometimes the most liberating thing is to say "no, I'm not there yet" and mean it. Sometimes the deepest recovery happens when you finally give yourself permission to be exactly where you are, without the weight of should.

I'll leave you with this. The opposite of forgiveness is not unforgiveness. The opposite of forgiveness is honesty. And honesty, real honesty about what happened and how you feel about it, is the foundation that recovery is actually built on.

You don't have to forgive. You just have to be honest. And that's hard enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system using polyvagal theory.
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker - A resource for understanding and recovering from complex trauma.
  • Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection through mindfulness and compassion.
  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery, exploring the stages of healing.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
